metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the `--restore` mode: specify a unit test (mock POST) that asserts re-posting a backed-up ADF body for a deleted comment id succeeds and that only ids present in the deletions JSONL are re-posted, plus a proving command (dry-run `--restore <backup.json> --ticket <key>` with `--dry-run` prints the re-POST list without issuing calls).
evidence: "Concrete restore procedure: a --restore <backup.json> --ticket <key> script mode re-POSTs each DELETED comment's ADF body"
No AC in the Acceptance Criteria section mentions --restore, re-POST behavior, or a test that restore re-creates correct content
"Jira comment deletion cannot be undone via API. The pre-deletion backup JSON... is the restore artifact" — the restore path is load-bearing for the safety plan but unverified
findings.finding: The `--restore` mode is described as a concrete rollback mechanism in the Rollback section (with a specific CLI flag `--restore <backup.json> --ticket <key>`, re-POST logic, and cross-reference to the deletions JSONL log) but has no corresponding acceptance criterion, unit test, or proving command. An executor cannot determine whether restore must be implemented and verified, and the safety claim ('restore artifact') is unverifiable.
findings.location: Rollback section ("Concrete restore procedure: a --restore <backup.json> --ticket <key> script mode...") and Acceptance Criteria (no restore AC present)
scenarios: Executor ships the script without implementing --restore because no AC requires it — the rollback path silently does not exist
An accidental over-deletion occurs and the restore mode fails (e.g. wrong ADF format on re-POST) with no prior test evidence of correctness
A reviewer approving the plan cannot verify the restore mechanism is correct because there is no proving command or unit test
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] Restore dry-run: `python cleanup.py --restore artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json --ticket REB-155 --dry-run` prints the list of comment bodies to be re-POSTed (cross-referenced against deletions-<ts>.jsonl) and exits 0 with zero POST calls. A unit test asserts that (a) only comment ids present in the deletions log are scheduled for restore, and (b) a simulated POST 201 is treated as success while a 4xx aborts with a non-zero exit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion covering the `--restore` mode: a test (unit or integration) asserts that given a backup JSON and a deletions log, `--restore` re-POSTs only the comments recorded as deleted for the specified ticket, and that re-running `--restore` for an already-restored comment is idempotent (no duplicate post).
evidence: Rollback section: 'a --restore <backup.json> --ticket <key> script mode re-POSTs each DELETED comment's ADF body… cross-referencing the deletions-<ts>.jsonl log to restore only what THIS run deleted'
AC section: covers backup, dry-run, deletion filter, rate-limit, completeness-gate, accountId cross-check, approval-gate, quiescing, post-execution state, deletion-log — no AC for --restore mode.
findings.finding: The 'Rollback' section describes `--restore <backup.json> --ticket <key>` as a concrete script mode (a deliverable), but no Acceptance Criterion covers it — neither a functional test nor a proving command verifies that the restore path re-creates comments correctly.
findings.location: ## What / Rollback subsection ('--restore <backup.json> --ticket <key> script mode') vs. Acceptance Criteria (no restore AC)
scenarios: The restore mode is implemented incorrectly (wrong log cross-reference, wrong ticket scoping, or double-posting) and there is no AC gate to catch it before it is needed in a real rollback scenario.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC criteria and unit tests for the `--restore` mode covering: (a) only comments listed in the matched deletions-*.jsonl are re-POSTed (not all backup entries), (b) missing/corrupt deletions log produces a clean non-zero exit before any POST, (c) a POST failure mid-restore is logged and the run continues (or aborts cleanly) without silent data loss, and (d) `--restore` with no matching deletions log for the given ticket exits with a clear error.
evidence: 'a --restore <backup.json> --ticket <key> script mode re-POSTs each DELETED comment's ADF body... cross-referencing the deletions-<ts>.jsonl log to restore only what THIS run deleted'
ABSENCE: the AC section lists seven unit/operator-attested criteria but none cover the restore mode's behavior, failure paths, or correct cross-referencing of the deletions log
findings.finding: The `--restore` mode is a new, distinct operator-facing CLI flow with non-trivial logic (cross-reference deletions-*.jsonl to re-POST only what THIS run deleted, handle partial-deletion state, handle POST failure mid-restore) but has zero corresponding unit tests or AC criteria. A caller invoking `--restore` on a corrupted/missing deletions log, a partial backup, or a mid-restore network failure gets undefined behavior with no test safety net.
findings.location: Rollback section ('--restore <backup.json> --ticket <key> script mode') and Acceptance Criteria (no restore test present)
scenarios: Operator invokes --restore with a deletions log from a different run — script silently restores wrong comments
Deletions log is corrupted/missing — restore path crashes without a clean error
POST fails mid-restore — no test asserts partial-restore state is handled or retried
Restore is invoked on a ticket not in the original 13 — undefined behavior
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--restore` section to the AC checklist with at least: a unit test that mocks the POST boundary and asserts only the intersection of (backup entries ∩ deletions-log entries for this run) are re-POSTed; a test that a missing deletions log produces exit code non-zero with no POSTs issued; and a test that a mid-restore POST 4xx/5xx is logged and counted in a restore-failure artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete outcome value vocabulary for the deletions JSONL 'outcome' field (e.g. `"deleted"`, `"404_skipped"`, `"retry_exhausted"`, `"dry_run"`) and specify which values the `--restore` mode treats as 'this run deleted this comment' vs. 'skip on restore'.
evidence: Plan: 'artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletions-<ts>.jsonl (one JSON object per line: ticket, comment id, normalized-body hash, outcome)'
Plan: '--restore <backup.json> --ticket <key> script mode re-POSTs each DELETED comment's ADF body ... cross-referencing the deletions-<ts>.jsonl log to restore only what THIS run deleted'
ABSENCE: no enumeration of outcome values (e.g. 'deleted' / '404_treated_as_success' / 'retry_exhausted' / 'dry_run_skipped') appears anywhere in the plan
The restore filter decision rule depends on parsing 'outcome' to identify which entries represent actual deletions vs. 404-skips vs. failures
findings.finding: The deletions JSONL schema field 'outcome' has an undefined value vocabulary. The plan specifies the log schema as '(ticket, comment id, normalized-body hash, outcome)' but never enumerates what values 'outcome' can take (e.g. 'deleted', '404_skipped', 'retry_exhausted', 'error'). This is load-bearing: the --restore mode cross-references the deletions log to 'restore only what THIS run deleted', which requires the restore filter to distinguish 'deleted' entries from 'skipped' or 'failed' ones — but without a defined outcome vocabulary, the restore protocol is underspecified.
findings.location: Rate limiting + transient-failure handling section / Rollback section / AC: 'The deletion-log artifact ... (one JSON object per line: ticket, comment id, normalized-body hash, outcome)'
scenarios: Operator runs --restore after a partial failure: the restore script must filter the log for 'deleted' entries, but without a defined outcome vocabulary it cannot distinguish a successfully-deleted comment from a 404-skipped or retry-exhausted one — leading to either over-restore (re-posting already-present comments) or under-restore (missing comments that were actually deleted).
An executor implementing the script must invent the outcome vocabulary, risking incompatibility between the deletion logger and the restore reader.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a schema table to the plan, e.g.: outcome values: `'deleted'` (HTTP 204 received), `'404_skipped'` (HTTP 404 on DELETE — already gone, counted as success), `'retry_exhausted'` (all retries failed, comment NOT confirmed deleted). The `--restore` mode re-POSTs only entries where outcome in `{'deleted', '404_skipped'}` — since 404 on DELETE means the comment was already absent, treating it as deleted is safe for restore purposes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `--restore` mode's non-happy-path: define retry behavior for failed re-POSTs, require a restore-log artifact (parallel to deletions-*.jsonl) that records per-comment POST outcome, assert non-zero exit and a clear operator message when any re-POST fails, and document how to re-run restore for the remaining gaps.
evidence: "a `--restore <backup.json> --ticket <key>` script mode re-POSTs each DELETED comment's ADF body"
"Target state after restore: the ticket again holds one instance of each restored distinct body"
No mention of retry behavior, partial-restore logging, error exit codes, or operator-visible failure signal for the restore path
findings.finding: The `--restore` mode's non-happy-path is unspecified: if a re-POST (`POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment`) fails (network error, Jira 4xx/5xx, or ADF rejection) during restore, the plan gives no guidance on exit behavior, partial-restore detection, or whether the operator is informed which comments were successfully re-created vs. dropped — leaving an incomplete restore indistinguishable from a complete one.
findings.location: Rollback section: `--restore <backup.json> --ticket <key>` script mode description
scenarios: Operator runs --restore after accidental over-deletion; a Jira rate-limit or transient 5xx drops 10 of 76 re-POSTs silently; the script exits 0; the ticket now has 66/76 comments with no artifact indicating which 10 are missing and no guidance on how to detect or retry the gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: the restore mode writes `artifacts/jira-cleanup/restore-<ts>.jsonl` (one line per comment: ticket, original-id, outcome, new-id-if-created); exits non-zero with a summary if any POST failed; and routes re-POSTs through the same `_call_with_retry` path used for DELETEs. Re-running `--restore` is idempotent (skip comments already present by normalized-body match).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `--restore` is idempotent: before re-POSTing a comment, check whether a comment with the same normalized body (and RECONCILER_MARKER) already exists on the ticket, and skip if so; assert the post-rollback Jira state (comment count equals `len(backup[key])` for fully-deleted tickets, or `pre-delete total - net-deletes + net-restores` for partial) and surface this count to the operator.
evidence: Plan states: 'cross-referencing the deletions-<ts>.jsonl log to restore only what THIS run deleted' — but does not guard against running --restore twice, which would POST each comment a second time.

Plan states: 'rollback is a safety net for accidental over-deletion, not a bit-exact undo' — acknowledges imperfection but makes no assertion about post-rollback Jira state correctness or restore idempotency.

T11 overlay: 'POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE: the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0 — a rollback can exit 0 while leaving the schema in an incorrect intermediate state.'
findings.finding: The --restore rollback mode is not specified as idempotent: re-running --restore after a successful (or partial) restore would re-POST already-restored comments, recreating duplicates in Jira. The plan also does not assert the Jira ticket state after a partial-failure restore — how many comments will exist, whether the reconciler marker will cause re-inflation on the next sync pass. The T11 POST-ROLLBACK STATE requirement is unmet: the plan says rollback 'exits 0' and 're-creates content', but does not assert the resulting Jira state is correct (no over-count, no under-count) for either a full or partial rollback.
findings.location: Rollback section: '--restore <backup.json> --ticket <key> script mode re-POSTs each DELETED comment's ADF body'
scenarios: Operator runs --restore, it completes. Operator mistakenly runs --restore again. Each deleted comment is now posted twice to Jira — new duplicates created, partially re-inflating the ticket toward the cap.

Partial-failure restore: --restore posts 200 of 4900 comments then fails. Operator re-runs --restore; the 200 already-restored comments are posted again (duplicated). Post-rollback state has more comments than intended.

After restore, the reconciler sees 're-added' comments with new IDs that carry RECONCILER_MARKER; the differ may interpret them differently than the originals, potentially triggering re-adds or no-ops incorrectly.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-POST existence check in --restore: fetch the ticket's current comment list, compute normalized bodies of extant marked comments, and skip re-POSTing any body that is already present. After restore completes, assert `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` total equals the expected count (original minus remaining duplicates) and log the delta. Document this idempotency guarantee in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling policy for non-retryable HTTP errors (403, 400) during DELETE: define whether the run aborts the entire ticket, skips and logs the failed comment id, or halts the whole script — and add a unit test asserting the chosen behavior.
evidence: 'the existing `_direct_rest_delete` -> `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` path does NOT retry HTTP 429/5xx — it re-raises 4xx/5xx immediately'
'SINGLE chosen route: wrap each DELETE call through `dispatch_one._call_with_retry`, which is the repo's one existing 429+Retry-After-aware retry'
No policy stated for 403/400/other non-retryable errors during DELETE — abort run? skip comment and log? halt ticket? unspecified.
findings.finding: Non-retryable HTTP errors (e.g., 403 Forbidden, 400 Bad Request) during the DELETE loop have no specified handling: the plan routes DELETEs through `_call_with_retry` which handles 429/Retry-After, but deterministic 4xx errors will propagate as unhandled exceptions, leaving the run in an undefined state (partial deletions with no clean abort/skip/continue policy).
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — Rate limiting + transient-failure handling; AC: Rate-limit safety
scenarios: Bot account loses delete permission mid-run (403) on one comment — run crashes mid-ticket leaving partial deletions with no documented recovery posture.
A comment id becomes invalid between fetch and delete (400) — exception propagates, JSONL may be incomplete, operator has no guidance on whether to re-run or investigate.
findings.suggested_fix: On a non-retryable 4xx (not 404): log the error with full context to the JSONL artifact (outcome: 'failed', reason: status+body), skip that comment, and continue — then exit non-zero at the end if any failures occurred, so the operator can re-inspect the specific ids. Add a unit test asserting a 403 is logged-and-skipped (not silently dropped and not run-aborting).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Rollback section to state that the restored ADF body already contains the reconciler marker (since it is captured in the backup), and that the reconciler will recognize the restored comment as already mirrored — no re-append occurs on the next sync.
evidence: Rollback section: 'the reconciler marker is re-appended by the normal outbound decorate path on the next sync'

Bot-authored identification section: the marker is appended to every outbound comment as its own ADF paragraph and 'survives the ADF round-trip and re-renders to the literal marker text' — i.e., it is present in the stored ADF body that gets backed up and re-POSTed.
If the restored ADF body already contains the marker, the reconciler sees a marked comment and does NOT re-append it — the description is incorrect.
findings.finding: The Rollback section claims 'the reconciler marker is re-appended by the normal outbound decorate path on the next sync' after a restore, but the described restore mechanism re-POSTs the raw ADF body from backup — which already contains the embedded marker (since the backup captures the full ADF body as stored in Jira). This creates a terminology/logic contradiction: the marker is not absent from the restored comment and does not need to be re-appended; it will already be present, and the reconciler will correctly detect it as 'already mirrored'. The phrase misleads implementers about restore behavior.
findings.location: ## What / Rollback subsection — 'the reconciler marker is re-appended by the normal outbound decorate path on the next sync'
scenarios: An implementer writing the restore path believes they must strip the marker before re-POSTing (to let the reconciler re-add it), inadvertently creating unmarked comments that the cleanup script would then not recognize as bot-authored in a future run.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the reconciler marker is re-appended by the normal outbound decorate path on the next sync' with 'the restored ADF body already contains the embedded reconciler marker (as captured in the backup), so the reconciler recognizes restored comments as already mirrored and does not re-add them.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `--restore` marker behavior: specify whether re-POSTed ADF bodies include or exclude the RECONCILER_MARKER, verify that the chosen approach leaves the reconciler in idempotent steady state (zero re-adds on next pass), and add a unit test asserting a restored comment is not re-mirrored by the reconciler.
evidence: 'a `--restore <backup.json> --ticket <key>` script mode re-POSTs each DELETED comment's ADF body'
'the reconciler marker is re-appended by the normal outbound decorate path on the next sync'
'_decorate_outbound_comment (`:140-147`) as its own paragraph — which survives the ADF round-trip'
Contradiction: backup ADF bodies contain the marker (it survives round-trip), yet the plan implies the marker is absent from restored comments and will be re-added by the reconciler.
findings.finding: The `--restore` mode's interaction with the RECONCILER_MARKER is underspecified and potentially self-defeating: the backup captures full ADF bodies which contain the embedded marker (added by `_decorate_outbound_comment`). If `--restore` re-POSTs these bodies verbatim, the reconciler will correctly identify restored comments as already-mirrored. But the plan states 'the reconciler marker is re-appended by the normal outbound decorate path on the next sync,' implying the restore POST omits the marker — in that case the reconciler treats restored comments as un-mirrored local comments and attempts to mirror them, potentially re-creating duplicates on the just-cleaned tickets.
findings.location: Rollback section — 'Concrete restore procedure'; 'Target state after restore'
scenarios: Operator triggers `--restore` after accidental over-deletion; restored comments lack the marker (if restore strips it) → reconciler sees them as un-mirrored → creates a new marked copy alongside the restored unmarked copy → two copies per comment → partial re-inflation.
Restored comments retain the marker (verbatim ADF re-POST) → reconciler correctly skips them → no duplication. But this contradicts the plan's stated behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Re-POST the verbatim backup ADF body (marker included) so the reconciler's `_normalize_comment_body` recognizes it as already-mirrored and emits zero re-adds. Remove the contradictory statement about the marker being 're-appended on next sync' — it should already be present in the restored body.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the DELETE loop's failure policy: define whether a `RetryExhaustedError` or non-retryable non-404 HTTP error on a single DELETE (a) aborts the entire run with a non-zero exit and retains the partial log, (b) logs the failure as `outcome='retry_exhausted'`/`'error'` and continues to the next DELETE, or (c) triggers a different operator-intervention flow.
evidence: Plan: 'wrap each DELETE call through dispatch_one._call_with_retry, which is the repo's one existing 429+Retry-After-aware retry'
dispatch_one.py:165-177: on exhaustion, HTTPError is re-raised raw; TimeoutError/JiraAPIError raise RetryExhaustedError
ABSENCE: no plan text specifies the script's behavior when _call_with_retry raises (abort loop? skip entry and continue? halt?)
Plan mentions resumability ('On partial failure, re-run --dry-run') but does not specify what constitutes a 'partial failure' exit vs. a complete abort
findings.finding: The DELETE loop's behavior on retry exhaustion is unspecified. The plan routes DELETEs through `dispatch_one._call_with_retry`, which on exhaustion either re-raises the raw `HTTPError` (for HTTP errors) or raises `RetryExhaustedError` (for TimeoutError/JiraAPIError per dispatch_one.py:165-177). The plan does not specify whether the script should (a) abort the entire run and leave remaining deletions unexecuted, (b) log the failure and continue to the next DELETE, or (c) halt and require manual intervention. This is a missing fallback for a concretely-specified sub-step.
findings.location: Rate limiting + transient-failure handling section
scenarios: A transient Jira outage causes retry exhaustion mid-run: without a defined policy, one implementer aborts (losing the partially-written log context) while another skips and continues (potentially leaving the log in a state where 'outcome' for failed entries is unclear for restore purposes).
A 403 Forbidden (non-retryable 4xx) is raised mid-run by _call_with_retry immediately — the plan says 404 is treated as success but does not address other non-retryable 4xx like 403.
findings.suggested_fix: Recommend option (b): log the failure entry with `outcome='retry_exhausted'` or `outcome='error:<status>'`, continue to the next DELETE, and at the end exit non-zero if any entry has a failure outcome — preserving the resumability guarantee while making the partial-failure artifact unambiguous for the restore procedure.

findings.finding: The approval gate between --dry-run and --execute lacks any enforcement mechanism. The plan states '--execute is required to delete and runs ONLY after explicit human approval of the dry-run diff' but never specifies how that approval is recorded or enforced — no --approved-by flag, no interactive y/n confirmation prompt, no file-based gate. An operator could run --execute directly without reviewing the dry-run diff, bypassing the only human-safety checkpoint before ~64k+ irreversible deletions.
findings.location: What — irreversible; execute ONLY under this T4 safety plan / Dry-run default bullet; AC: 'Dry-run diff … is produced and human-approved before --execute. [operator-attested]'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a mandatory interactive confirmation prompt when --execute is passed: print the dry-run summary, then require the operator to type a confirmation phrase (e.g. 'DELETE 13 TICKETS') before any deletion begins. Alternatively, require `--execute --dry-run-artifact=<path>` where the path must point to a previously written dry-run JSON, proving the dry-run was run and the artifact was produced.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean, robust

verify command `wc -l artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletions-<ts>.jsonl` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

evidence: file_impact is empty
findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing to 10 req/s, perform a short empirical probe: issue a burst of ~20 DELETE or GET requests against a non-critical Jira ticket and inspect the response headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, Retry-After) to derive an evidence-based throughput cap; document the measured limit and resulting estimated run duration in the plan.
evidence: 'Throttle to a conservative default (≤10 req/s, i.e. ≥100ms inter-call sleep)' — no citation of Jira's documented rate-limit for this tenant
'~64k+ DELETEs (≈4900 × 13)' — at 10 req/s this is ~107 minutes minimum; the plan does not acknowledge this duration or validate it is operationally acceptable
No prior probe or measured Jira response headers (e.g., X-RateLimit-*) cited
findings.finding: The DELETE throughput cap of ≤10 req/s (≥100ms sleep) is described as a 'conservative default' but is asserted rather than derived from Jira Cloud's actual per-tenant rate-limit policy or measured behavior; Jira Cloud enforces tenant-specific and plan-specific rate limits that may be lower (causing 429 storms) or could permit higher throughput (unnecessarily extending a multi-hour window).
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling'
scenarios: Actual tenant rate limit is 5 req/s — the script hits sustained 429s, Retry-After stalls extend the window to 3+ hours, and the reconciler-quiesce window is exceeded mid-run.
Actual tenant rate limit is 50 req/s — the 10 req/s cap unnecessarily extends the run to 107 minutes when 20 minutes was achievable.

findings.finding: The plan describes a rollback path ('re-POST its body to the ticket') but does not assert the TARGET STATE of Jira after a rollback, as required by T11. Specifically: after a partial execution that deleted N comments before aborting, re-POSTing all backed-up comments from those tickets would push them back toward or to the 5000-comment cap — re-creating the very problem being solved. The plan does not define (a) WHICH comments should be re-posted on rollback (all? only the deleted ones?), (b) what the acceptable post-rollback comment count is, or (c) how to determine which backed-up comments are still present vs already deleted, making a safe selective restore operationally undefined.
findings.location: ## What — irreversible; execute ONLY under this T4 safety plan / 'Rollback (irreversible-op mitigation)' sub-section; AC: 'Deletion log artifact'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a rollback procedure section: 'On partial-run abort, use the deletion log (ticket + deleted comment ids) to identify exactly which comments were deleted. Re-POST only those deleted comments (body from backup JSON, matched by id). Expected post-rollback state: comment count = pre-run total minus already-deleted count; if pre-run total was 5000, post-rollback count will be above the point reached mid-run. Verify with GET /comment?maxResults=0 total field. Do NOT re-POST the full backup — that would return the ticket to 5000.' Also add a post-rollback AC: 'After rollback, each affected ticket has comment count = (pre-run total) − (count in deletion log for that ticket), confirmed by metadata call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before building the completeness-gate logic, add an empirical probe step: issue `GET /rest/api/3/issue/REB-155/comment?maxResults=0` against the live Jira instance, inspect the response envelope, and confirm `total` is present and equals the known 5000; record the observed response shape in the plan or a fixture.
evidence: 'GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0 returns {total, maxResults, startAt, comments: []}; the script reads total from that envelope (new script-level work, not provided by the reuse target)'
The unit test for the completeness gate mocks the API — it does not validate the actual Jira response shape
No mention of a prior probe or reference to Jira REST v3 documentation confirming maxResults=0 returns total
findings.finding: The completeness-gate design assumes that `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` returns a JSON envelope containing a `total` field. This is asserted as fact but is an unverified assumption about live Jira Cloud behavior for this tenant and API version; the unit tests mock it rather than validate it against the real API.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / 'Pagination' section; AC line 'Backup artifact exists and passes its per-ticket completeness check'
scenarios: Jira Cloud returns maxResults=0 with an empty response, or omits total, or returns total=0 — the completeness gate reads 0, compares to len(fetched)=5000, refuses to proceed, and the cleanup cannot run.
Jira Cloud paginates differently for maxResults=0 (e.g., ignores it, returns first page) — total reflects only the first page, undermining the guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line probe task: `curl -u ... 'https://<host>/rest/api/3/issue/REB-155/comment?maxResults=0' | python -m json.tool` — confirm `total` field present and == 5000 before wiring the completeness gate. Alternatively, use `maxResults=1` which is more widely documented to return total in the envelope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan that the cleanup script directly couples to private-API functions (`_normalize_comment_body`, `_call_with_retry`), and add a mitigation: either pin the import with an inline comment warning ('PRIVATE API — coordinate changes with jira-cleanup script') in the source modules, or add a regression test that imports the cleanup's normalization path and the reconciler's path and asserts they produce identical output for a shared fixture.
evidence: 'Group candidate comments by outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body(body) — imported and reused verbatim, NOT re-implemented'
'wrap each DELETE call through dispatch_one._call_with_retry'
Both functions are private (underscore-prefixed), signaling no stable interface contract; no interface or adapter layer is proposed
findings.finding: The cleanup script imports private implementation details (`outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body`, `dispatch_one._call_with_retry`) from production modules across component boundaries; if either private function's signature or semantics changes (e.g., normalization adds a new strip step), the cleanup script's dedup key silently diverges from the reconciler's, potentially misidentifying 'duplicates,' with no interface contract protecting against this.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / 'Dedup key' and 'Rate limiting' sections
scenarios: A future change to _normalize_comment_body (e.g., stripping additional noise tokens for P2) is made without updating the cleanup script — the cleanup script on re-run computes different dedup groups, keeping different 'oldest' copies or mislabeling unique comments as duplicates.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single cross-check test: `assert cleanup_normalize(body) == outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body(body)` for a set of representative ADF bodies — this acts as a canary if normalization drifts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a file-based lockfile (e.g., `artifacts/jira-cleanup/cleanup.lock`) acquired at script startup and released on exit, with an explicit error if the lock is already held, to prevent concurrent `--execute` instances from interleaving deletions on the same tickets.
evidence: 'Re-running is safe: a comment already deleted (404 on DELETE) is treated as success'
'the keep-one grouping is recomputed from the live paginated set each run'
No mention of a lockfile, semaphore, or operator process-level guard against concurrent instances.
ABSENCE: no mechanism prevents two simultaneous `--execute` runs from interleaving their DELETE calls.
findings.finding: No concurrent-execution guard is specified: if an operator inadvertently launches a second script instance against the same 13 tickets while the first is mid-run (e.g., impatient re-run, CI re-trigger), both instances independently compute delete-lists from the same live paginated state, and their DELETE calls can race — including one instance deleting the 'kept oldest' comment that the other instance has already committed to preserving, resulting in zero copies of a distinct body on that ticket (data loss beyond what either run intended).
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — Idempotent + resumable; What — T4 safety plan
scenarios: Operator runs `--execute`, waits 30s, sees no output (rate-limit stall), re-runs in a second terminal — both instances proceed; first deletes comment id X as a duplicate, second had computed X as the 'kept oldest' (because another copy was deleted between its fetch and first instance's deletes) and now has no copy to keep.
findings.suggested_fix: At startup (before backup fetch), attempt to acquire an exclusive lock via `fcntl.flock` or a PID lockfile under `artifacts/jira-cleanup/cleanup.lock`; if acquisition fails, print a clear message ('Another cleanup instance is running; if stale, delete the lockfile') and exit non-zero.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test asserting that when a DELETE returns HTTP 404 it is recorded as 'success/already-deleted' in the deletions log (not as an error), and that a re-run computes a delete list only from comments still present in the live paginated fetch (not from the previous run's log), so no comment is double-logged or double-attempted.
evidence: 'Re-running is safe: a comment already deleted (404 on DELETE) is treated as success.'
'On partial failure, re-run --dry-run to see the remaining duplicates and resume --execute'
ABSENCE: no AC item asserts 404-on-DELETE is treated as success or that a re-run after partial failure issues no spurious DELETEs for already-deleted comments
findings.finding: The idempotent-resume behavior (re-run after partial failure treats 404-on-DELETE as success and recomputes the delete list from the live paginated set) is described in prose but has no unit test or AC criterion. This is a new behavioral property of the `--execute` path whose failure would silently mis-report success or double-count deletes.
findings.location: 'Idempotent + resumable' prose section; Acceptance Criteria (no resumability test present)
scenarios: A 404 response during deletion is mishandled as a hard failure, causing the run to abort and leaving the ticket partially cleaned with no clean resume path
A re-run after partial failure attempts to re-delete already-deleted comments and spuriously logs them as 'deleted' in the artifact, inflating the deletion count

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the keep-one grouping is computed once (upfront for all 13 tickets) or re-fetched per-ticket immediately before that ticket's deletions begin; if upfront, add a positive rationale for why the single fetch is sufficient (e.g., guaranteed full quiescing) or switch to per-ticket re-fetch to tighten the TOCTOU window.
evidence: 'Reuse the live-validated acli.py::get_comments(key) with --paginate + _parse_paginated_comments … to build the full comment set for backup + delete-list.'

'On partial failure, re-run --dry-run to see the remaining duplicates and resume --execute; the keep-one grouping is recomputed from the live paginated set each run.' — this only applies on full re-runs, not mid-run per-ticket refreshes.

AC: 'Reconciler quiescing during --execute: the run window is coordinated so the live reconciler does not write to the 13 tickets mid-deletion … [operator-attested]' — operator-attestation with no technical enforcement.
Plan does not state a positive rationale for the single upfront fetch vs. per-ticket re-fetch approach.
findings.finding: The plan computes the keep-one grouping (oldest-per-distinct-body, delete-list) once from the upfront paginated fetch of all 13 tickets and then executes deletions sequentially over potentially hours — creating a snapshot-staleness window. If the reconciler is not fully quiesced and posts even one new comment between the initial fetch and the deletion of that ticket's duplicates, the script operates on a stale delete list for that ticket. Because the plan's sole safeguard is operator-attested quiescing (no enforcement mechanism), a per-ticket re-fetch immediately before starting each ticket's deletions would tighten the TOCTOU window from O(total run duration) to O(single-ticket delete time) with no additional complexity. The plan provides no rationale for why a single upfront fetch (rather than per-ticket re-fetch) is preferred.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — Pagination section / What — T4 safety plan / AC: Reconciler quiescing during --execute
scenarios: Reconciler passes happen every N minutes; if quiescing coordination fails (operator error, pass cadence resumes unexpectedly), a new bot comment arrives between the upfront fetch and that ticket's deletions — the script deletes using a stale snapshot.
With 13 tickets × ~4900 deletions each at ≥100ms/call, the full --execute window is ~107 minutes; quiescing for that entire window is operationally burdensome and easy to miscalculate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a per-ticket re-fetch step: immediately before deleting ticket K's duplicates, re-fetch K's current comments (one `get_comments(K)` call) and recompute K's delete list from the fresh snapshot. This tightens the staleness window to < the time it takes to delete one ticket's duplicates (~8 minutes per ticket at 10 req/s) and removes dependence on perfect operator quiescing for the entire run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body` (and its `_load_adf()` lazy-loader) is importable and functional from the cleanup script's execution context (outside the reconciler package); add a startup smoke-test import assertion to the script, or document the required `sys.path` / install mode.
evidence: Plan: 'Group candidate comments by outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body(body) — imported and reused verbatim, NOT re-implemented'

outbound_comments.py:1-45: module docstring notes 'the reconciler modules are spec-loaded under test, where `from . import` does not resolve' — the lazy-loader is specifically designed for the reconciler's in-package context

outbound_comments.py:134: `_load_adf().adf_to_text(body)` — `_load_adf` uses `importlib.util` with sibling path resolution tied to `__file__` of `outbound_comments.py`; a cleanup script importing this would need the reconciler package on `sys.path` and the sibling `adf.py` resolvable from `outbound_comments.__file__`
No test or prior usage of `_normalize_comment_body` from outside the reconciler package exists in the codebase
findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_comments._normalize_comment_body` (and its internal `_load_adf()` lazy-loader) can be 'imported and reused verbatim' in the standalone cleanup script, but `_load_adf()` uses `importlib.util` with sibling-relative path resolution designed for the reconciler's internal package context. Whether this resolves correctly when invoked from a script outside the reconciler package is an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Dedup key = the reconciler's OWN normalization'; outbound_comments.py:116-137
scenarios: The cleanup script imports `_normalize_comment_body` from outside the reconciler package; `_load_adf()` resolves `adf.py` relative to `outbound_comments.__file__` — if the reconciler package is installed (not editable), or the working directory differs, the sibling path resolution may fail with `ModuleNotFoundError`, causing the entire dedup-key computation to crash at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a startup guard in the cleanup script: `from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler.outbound_comments import _normalize_comment_body; _ = _normalize_comment_body('smoke')` and confirm it succeeds before proceeding. Alternatively, since `adf.adf_to_text` and RECONCILER_MARKER are both importable directly (`from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler.adf import adf_to_text; from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler.outbound_comments import RECONCILER_MARKER`), the cleanup script could import those two primitives directly and replicate the two-line normalization inline — which is legitimate given it IS the same logic, just avoiding the lazy-loader indirection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Validate the `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` → `{total}` behavior against a live Jira Cloud instance (e.g., against one of the 13 capped tickets) before relying on it as the completeness gate; document the probe result and add a fallback if the endpoint returns `total: 0`.

findings.finding: The completeness-gate uses `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` to read the Jira-reported `total` for each ticket. This is a new REST endpoint surface not currently exercised anywhere in the codebase (confirmed by search). While this endpoint is standard Jira Cloud REST API v3 behavior, the plan does not cite a probe, live validation, or code reference confirming the `?maxResults=0` pattern returns `{total, comments: []}` for this specific Jira Cloud instance. The evidence class is Partially-verified (documented but unprobed in-environment).
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — Pagination section; AC line 'Backup artifact exists and passes its per-ticket completeness check — `len(fetched)` from the paginated `get_comments` equals `total` read from the dedicated `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` envelope'
scenarios: The Jira Cloud instance or a corporate API gateway returns a different envelope shape (e.g., wraps under 'values' key rather than 'comments' key, or omits 'total' at maxResults=0), causing the completeness gate to always abort with a false mismatch.
The bot account lacks read scope on the comment count endpoint (unlikely but unverified), causing an unexpected 403 that blocks the backup phase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan an explicit step specifying how the cleanup script instantiates `AcliClient` (or equivalent credential context) so that `dispatch_one._call_with_retry(client._direct_rest_delete, path)` can be correctly wired for the DELETE loop.
evidence: dispatch_one.py:104: def _call_with_retry(fn, *args, ...) — wraps an arbitrary callable
acli_rest.py:304: def _direct_rest_delete(self, path) — instance method on AcliClient/AcliRestMixin
Plan says 'wrap each DELETE call through dispatch_one._call_with_retry' without specifying how client._direct_rest_delete is obtained in the new script context
findings.finding: The plan instructs the cleanup script to route DELETE calls through `dispatch_one._call_with_retry`, but `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` wraps a callable `fn` — it does not itself issue HTTP requests. The DELETE transport is `AcliClient._direct_rest_delete`, an instance method. For `_call_with_retry` to wrap it, the cleanup script must have an `AcliClient` instance available. The plan never specifies constructing an `AcliClient` for the new script, leaving the binding between the retry wrapper and the DELETE transport under-specified.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Rate limiting section — 'wrap each DELETE call through dispatch_one._call_with_retry'
scenarios: Implementer writes _call_with_retry(client._direct_rest_delete, path) — works if client is initialized, but plan never says to create a client
Implementer hand-rolls a urllib DELETE call and tries to pass it directly — bypasses the existing transport and loses credential handling

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm and implement the restore path's comment re-POST mechanism: specify whether restore uses the existing `add_comment` (ACLI, plain-text — requires ADF→text rendering of the backup body before re-posting) or a new `_direct_rest_post_json` to `POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment` with the raw ADF dict; add the chosen helper to the plan's implementation spec and verify it against Jira Cloud docs.
evidence: Plan: 're-POSTs each DELETED comment's ADF body (`POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment`, cross-referencing the deletions-<ts>.jsonl log to restore only what THIS run deleted)'
acli_cli_ops.py:469-492: add_comment sends via ACLI subprocess with plain-text `--body` flag, not via REST POST with ADF
acli_rest.py:304-320: _direct_rest_delete exists but no _direct_rest_post_comment-with-ADF exists
Backup stores raw ADF dicts (body field from Jira REST GET response) — ADF dict cannot be sent as ACLI --body string
ABSENCE: no `POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment` helper found in any acli_*.py file
Evidence class: Unverified (new endpoint+ADF surface not exercised in codebase)
findings.finding: The restore path (`--restore` mode) specifies re-POSTing each deleted comment's ADF body via `POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment`. The backup captures raw ADF dicts as returned by Jira REST. However, no existing helper in the codebase performs a REST POST to the comment endpoint with an ADF body — `add_comment` routes through ACLI subprocess with a plain-text `--body` flag (acli_cli_ops.py:480-492), which then re-wraps the string as ADF via `text_to_adf`. Piping a raw ADF dict through the ACLI `--body` flag would serialize it as a Python repr string, not valid ADF, corrupting restored content. The new `POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment` REST surface is Unverified in the codebase and the ADF-vs-plaintext mismatch is unaddressed.
findings.location: Rollback (irreversible-op mitigation) section — `--restore <backup.json>` mode; AC line 're-POSTs each DELETED comment's ADF body (POST /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment)'
scenarios: Operator runs --restore after accidental over-deletion; restored comments arrive as garbled ADF-repr strings or the restore call fails entirely, defeating the rollback safety net.
Restore is invoked and routes through ACLI add_comment with an ADF dict serialized as `--body {'type': 'doc', ...}` — ACLI interprets this as a literal string body, not ADF, breaking formatting.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `adf.adf_to_text(backup_body)` to render the saved ADF dict back to plain text, then call the existing `client.add_comment(key, rendered_text)` (ACLI path). This reuses proven infrastructure with no new REST surface. Note the round-trip loses ADF formatting details but restores human-readable content — which the plan already acknowledges ('content-restore, NOT id-restore'). Alternatively, add a `_direct_rest_post_json(f'/rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment', {'body': adf_dict})` helper to AcliRestMixin and verify it returns 201 with the Jira Cloud comment API.

findings.finding: The deletion log artifact path is never named, unlike the backup artifact which has an explicit path (`artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json`), leaving the executor to invent a location and making AC6's proving command impossible to write without guessing.
findings.location: AC6: 'The deletion log artifact (ticket, comment id, body hash, outcome) exists for the executed run.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' checklist and AC6: 'Deletion log written to artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletions-<ts>.jsonl (one JSON line per deletion attempt: ticket, comment_id, body_hash, outcome).'

findings.finding: The pagination completeness check — `len(fetched) == total` where `total` comes from a separate `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` call — is a new safety-critical branching behavior (script refuses to proceed if they differ), but no unit test is specified for this branch. A bug in this check (e.g., off-by-one, wrong JSON key, or the check being skipped) would allow the script to proceed with a silently-truncated fetch and generate an incomplete/dangerous delete list.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Pagination' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria item 1

findings.finding: The secondary accountId cross-check behavior — 'the script asserts that all marked (candidate) comments across the 13 tickets resolve to a single bot accountId and records it — a divergent accountId on a marked comment aborts the run for manual review' — is described as a safety feature in Mechanisms but has no corresponding AC to verify it was implemented and fires correctly.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms > Bot-authored identification (secondary cross-check paragraph) vs Acceptance Criteria (all six ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 with a sub-clause: '(e) a divergent accountId on any marked comment triggers an abort before any deletion, verified by a unit test that injects a marked comment with a foreign accountId and asserts SystemExit / raised exception.'

findings.finding: The accountId-divergence abort path — where a marked comment resolves to an unexpected bot accountId and the run is aborted for manual review — is a new safety-critical branching behavior with no corresponding unit test specified anywhere in the plan. If this guard is broken (either fails to abort or aborts spuriously on valid data), the error would be silent until a manual run, potentially allowing deletion to proceed on a corrupt or misidentified comment set.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Secondary cross-check' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria — no test for this branch
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC item 3 unit test (or add a sibling test) with a synthetic comment set where one marked comment carries a different accountId; assert the script aborts pre-deletion. Add a complementary case where all marked comments share one accountId and assert the run proceeds to the dry-run/delete phase.

findings.finding: The plan specifies TWO co-equal, mutually exclusive implementation routes for the DELETE-loop 429/5xx retry layer — a new bespoke script-level wrapper ('the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper — new script-level work') AND an existing one ('alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry') — without a decision criterion for which to implement. This is a T8(b) processing-protocol gap: the decision rule governing which retry path governs the DELETE loop is absent, forcing the executor to make an arbitrary architectural choice between new untested code and a battle-tested existing wrapper.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section, 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the dual-option paragraph with a single directive: 'Route each DELETE through the existing `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, jira_key, comment_id)` (dispatch_one.py:104); this provides 429+Retry-After, 5xx jittered backoff, and exhaustion semantics with no new code. Insert a per-call `time.sleep(0.1)` throttle before the call for the ≤10 req/s budget.' This eliminates the bespoke-wrapper path entirely.

findings.finding: The rollback plan is verbally described ('re-POST its body to the ticket') but no restore script, command template, or concrete operator procedure is provided, making the back-out path notional rather than executable.
findings.location: Rollback (irreversible-op mitigation) section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--restore backup-<ts>.json --ticket REB-XXX` subcommand (or standalone restore script) that iterates the backup artifact for the named ticket and re-POSTs each comment body via the Jira REST API, logging each re-POSTed id. Document that restored comments receive new ids/timestamps (content-restore only).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the backup completeness proving command to independently assert per-ticket counts: e.g. `jq -e 'to_entries | all(.value | length == .)' artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json` is insufficient alone — add a command that reads the per-ticket expected totals (from a sidecar counts file emitted by the script) and asserts `jq '[to_entries[] | .value | length] | add' backup.json` equals the sum, or emit the per-ticket totals as a machine-readable sidecar and assert against it.
evidence: "Proving command: `python -m json.tool artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json >/dev/null` + the script's count-reconciliation log line per ticket"
The `python -m json.tool` command exits 0 for any valid JSON, regardless of whether per-ticket comment counts match the Jira-reported total
The count reconciliation log line is produced by the same process that performs the backup — it is not an independent check an operator can run after the fact
findings.finding: The backup completeness AC's proving command (`python -m json.tool artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json >/dev/null`) only verifies JSON parseability, not the count reconciliation claim (`len(backup[key]) == jira_reported_total` per ticket). The count check's evidence is only the script's own log line, which is not an independent verification command an operator can run post-hoc to confirm the backup is complete.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, first AC: 'Backup artifact exists and passes its per-ticket completeness check...' Proving command: `python -m json.tool ... >/dev/null` + 'the script's count-reconciliation log line per ticket'
scenarios: A bug silently truncates the fetch for one ticket; the backup JSON is valid and `python -m json.tool` passes; the count mismatch only appears in the script's own log, which may scroll past unnoticed
An operator reviewing the completed work runs the proving command, gets exit 0, and incorrectly concludes the backup is complete when it is not
findings.suggested_fix: Have the script emit a sidecar `artifacts/jira-cleanup/counts-<ts>.json` of the form `{"REB-155": 5000, ...}` (Jira-reported totals from the `?maxResults=0` call). Then the proving command becomes: `python verify_backup.py artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json artifacts/jira-cleanup/counts-<ts>.json` which runs `assert len(backup[key]) == counts[key] for key in counts` and exits non-zero on any mismatch — independently executable by any operator without re-running the full script.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact import path for the pagination reuse target — `acli_cli_ops.get_comments` (module-level, no client needed) vs. `AcliClient.get_comments` (instance method, requires credential-wired client) — and document how the cleanup script initializes whichever client/context is needed for both the paginated fetch and the DELETE REST calls.
evidence: acli.py:653 defines AcliClient.get_comments(self, jira_key) as an instance method requiring self._run()
acli_cli_ops.py:546 defines a module-level get_comments(jira_key) callable standalone
Plan names 'acli.py::get_comments(key)' without specifying client construction or credential wiring for the new standalone cleanup script
findings.finding: The plan references 'acli.py::get_comments(key)' as the pagination reuse target, but in the actual codebase there are TWO distinct `get_comments` symbols: (a) the module-level standalone function in `acli_cli_ops.py:546` (callable without a client instance) and (b) the `AcliClient.get_comments` instance method in `acli.py:653` (requires a fully-initialized `AcliClient` with credentials). The plan's `acli.py::get_comments` notation points to the instance method, which means the cleanup script must construct and configure an `AcliClient` — but the plan never specifies how the cleanup script obtains or initializes the client (credentials, Jira URL, ACLI CLI path). If the implementer instead uses the standalone `acli_cli_ops.get_comments`, it bypasses the client entirely but loses REST-credential context needed for the DELETE calls.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Pagination section — 'Reuse the live-validated acli.py::get_comments(key)'
scenarios: Implementer writes `from rebar_reconciler.acli_cli_ops import get_comments` (correct standalone) but plan said acli.py — notation mismatch creates confusion
Implementer instantiates AcliClient but plan provides no guidance on how to configure it (Jira URL, user, token) for a standalone script outside the normal reconciler pass
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'acli.py::get_comments(key)' with 'acli_cli_ops.get_comments(key)' (the standalone module-level function) for the fetch path, and specify that DELETE calls use AcliClient._direct_rest_delete (or a direct urllib call) wrapped in dispatch_one._call_with_retry, with a note on how the cleanup script loads credentials (e.g., same env-vars as the reconciler).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sixth bullet to the 'What' T4 safety-plan checklist for reconciler quiescing (pause pass cadence or choose an inter-pass window) so it is a required pre-execute step, not only an AC gate.

AC item 8: 'Reconciler quiescing during --execute: the run window is coordinated so the live reconciler does not write to the 13 tickets mid-deletion… [operator-attested]'
findings.finding: The 'What' T4 safety-plan checklist (5 bullet items) omits reconciler quiescing during --execute, yet AC item 8 explicitly requires it ('Reconciler quiescing during --execute: the run window is coordinated…'). An implementer following the safety plan step-by-step would not be reminded to quiesce the reconciler, creating a cross-section gap between the stated safety controls and the acceptance gate.
findings.location: ## What — irreversible; execute ONLY under this T4 safety plan (inlined) (5-bullet checklist) vs. Acceptance Criteria item 8 (Reconciler quiescing)
scenarios: Operator follows the safety-plan checklist step-by-step, completes all 5 bullets, and triggers --execute without pausing the reconciler — the reconciler writes to a ticket mid-deletion, potentially re-inflating or causing a race.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' checklist: '- [ ] **Reconciler quiescing:** coordinate the --execute window so no reconcile-bridge pass runs against the 13 tickets during deletion (pause the scheduled pass or execute between passes and confirm the prior pass completed before starting).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Route the `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` metadata call through the same `_call_with_retry` path (or equivalent 429/transient-error-aware retry) used for DELETEs, and specify the failure policy if the call fails after retries (abort the run for that ticket, refuse all deletions).
evidence: 'the script reads `total` from that envelope (new script-level work, not provided by the reuse target)'
'REFUSING to proceed if they differ (guards a silently-truncated fetch)'
No mention of retry or error handling for this metadata fetch — contrast with DELETE path which explicitly names `_call_with_retry`.
findings.finding: The metadata call `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` (used to obtain `total` for the completeness gate) is not routed through any retry mechanism, despite being an external Jira API call that gates the entire run. A transient network error or 429 on this call would cause an unhandled exception before any backup or deletion occurs, with no recovery path specified.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — Pagination section; AC: Backup artifact / Completeness-gate enforcement
scenarios: Jira returns 429 on the metadata call for one of the 13 tickets — unhandled exception aborts the pre-backup phase; operator sees a crash with no deletions and no clear re-run guidance.
Transient 500 on metadata call mid-loop (after some tickets backed up but before others) — script crashes with a partially-written backup artifact whose completeness is unclear.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-restore state-check step to the --restore mode: before re-POSTing any body from the backup, fetch the current live paginated comments for the ticket and skip re-posting any body whose normalized form already exists (idempotent restore); document this in the rollback section.
evidence: 'the reconciler marker is re-appended by the normal outbound decorate path on the next sync' — implies the reconciler WILL run after deletions are committed
AC: 'Reconciler quiescing during --execute' is scoped only to the execution window, not to the rollback window
No pre-restore state-check is specified to detect reconciler-re-added comments before re-POSTing from backup
findings.finding: The rollback (--restore) procedure does not account for the reconciler having already re-synced the deleted comments after the reconciler quiesce window is lifted. If rollback is needed after the reconciler has run at least one pass post-deletion, the reconciler will have re-added the missing comments; --restore would then re-POST those same bodies again, recreating duplicates (the exact problem being fixed).
findings.location: Rollback section / 'Concrete restore procedure'
scenarios: Operator runs --execute, unquiesces the reconciler, reconciler runs and re-syncs some of the now-deleted comments; operator then needs to rollback due to over-deletion; --restore re-POSTs the same bodies the reconciler just added, creating duplicates again.
findings.suggested_fix: In the --restore mode, call get_comments(key) + _normalize_comment_body on each live comment first; for each backup entry in deletions-<ts>.jsonl, skip re-POST if the normalized body is already present. Add a note to the rollback section: 'If the reconciler has run since execution, some deleted comments may already be re-synced; --restore detects and skips those to avoid re-duplication.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
